result:
# The Strange Comfort of Unfinished Things

There's something I find quietly fascinating about things left incomplete. The half-read book on a nightstand. The melody a street musician stops playing mid-phrase to adjust their instrument. The sentence someone begins and then—

We tend to treat incompleteness as failure, as evidence of poor follow-through or weak discipline. But I wonder if unfinished things hold a particular kind of energy that completed things simply cannot. A finished novel is sealed. It has declared itself. But the novel someone abandoned on page 247 still breathes with possibility. It could still go anywhere.

Psychologists call it the Zeigarnik effect — the brain's tendency to remember interrupted tasks more vividly than completed ones. We keep returning to the open loop, mentally circling it, which means unfinished things occupy more of our inner life than finished ones ever do. Completion, in a strange way, is a kind of forgetting.

I think about old buildings where construction clearly stopped decades ago. Rebar still reaching upward from concrete pillars like fingers expecting to hold something. These structures look like questions. Finished buildings are answers. And answers, useful as they are, tend to end conversations.

Maybe this is why certain music moves us — because a good song creates tension it only partially resolves. It finishes, technically, but leaves something hovering in the room afterward.

There might be wisdom in occasionally resisting the urge to close every loop. Some openings are worth preserving simply because they keep us
